coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of polyethylene glycol into polyethylene terephthalate towards blue emitting co-polyester
coredata.dc:creator: He, Xiaohong
Zhou, Xuefei
Jia, Kun
Zhang, Dawei
Shou, Hongguo
Liu, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, fluorescent co-polyester was prepared by incorporating polyethylene glycol (PEG) segment into backbone of polyethylene terephthalate (PET). The crystalline structures and fluorescence properties of obtained PET-PEG copolymers were systematically studied. It was found that the crystalline behaviour of PET was significantly alternated after incorporation of flexible PEG segment, meanwhile the ultrasmall sized carbon dots with blue emitting luminescence can be obtained from incorporated PEG segment, which contributed to the fluorescence property of resultant PET-PEG co-polyester solution and fiber.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethylene terephthalate
Polyethylene glycol
Carbon dots
Copolymer
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confirmation of carbon dots(CDs) formation via thermal decomposition of PEG

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs obtained for different refluxing time (a), the fluorescence emission spectra excited at different wavelengths of CDs obtained after refluxing for 0.5h (b) and 2.0h (c), shown in inset was a cuvette containing CDs solution under 365nm illumination, the emission spectra (excited at 365nm) of synthesized CDs obtained at different reaction time, shown inset was the variation of the emission wavelength versus reaction time (d).
Synthesis route of PET-PEG copolymer (a), DSC (b) and TGA (c) thermograms of PET and PET-PEG polymer.
The polarizing micrographs of PET (a), PET-PEG (b) and their XRD patterns (c).

The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PET-PEG copolymers in solution and solid state (A) and fluorescence microscope images of copolymer fiber at original (a, b, e, f), partially stretched (c, g) and completed crystallized (d, h) state (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Incorporation of polyethylene glycol into polyethylene terephthalate towards blue emitting co-polyester

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Branch of Advanced Functional Materials, High Temperature Resistant Polymer and Composites Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, School of Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Branch of Advanced Functional Materials, High Temperature Resistant Polymer and Composites Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, School of Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, fluorescent co-polyester was prepared by incorporating polyethylene glycol (PEG) segment into backbone of polyethylene terephthalate (PET). The crystalline structures and fluorescence properties of obtained PET-PEG copolymers were systematically studied. It was found that the crystalline behaviour of PET was significantly alternated after incorporation of flexible PEG segment, meanwhile the ultrasmall sized carbon dots with blue emitting luminescence can be obtained from incorporated PEG segment, which contributed to the fluorescence property of resultant PET-PEG co-polyester solution and fiber.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most typical thermoplastic polymer in polyester family, polyethylene terephthalate (PET) has been widely applied in various fields such as packaging, textile, and engineering composites due to its cost effectiveness, excellent moisture/gas barrier properties, mechanical strength, and good processability [1–3]. However, the applications of traditional PET polymers are limited because of low toughness which is derived from their rigid backbone molecular structures. Meanwhile, the optofunctionalization of PET would be of great importance for their potential application in advanced optoelectric devices, where transparent PET thermoplastics are currently employed as flexible substrate [4,5].

Carbon dots (CDs) have witnessed increasing research attentions owing to their unique superiorities such as low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and photoluminescence properties, etc [6–9]. Since the first report in 2004, CDs have been extensively used in the fields of bio-imaging, biochemical sensor and photo-catalysis [10–12]. Generally, the diverse protocols for CDs synthesis can be simply classified into two groups of top-down and bottom-up methods, respectively [11]. Although these investigates have made significant progress for the CDs synthesis, multiple steps and surface passivation treatment for improving the water solubility and fluorescence properties of obtained CDs are still involved in many protocols.

Herein, we discovered that fluorescent CDs can be obtained by direct thermal decomposition of polyethylene glycol (PEG) without using any surface passivation agent in nitrogen atmosphere above 250°C. More interestingly, we found that the highly transparent PET-PEG copolymers showing intense blue emission under UV light illumination can be synthesized by incorporating PEG segment into the backbone of PET, where fluorescent CDs are in-situ formulated and dispersed in obtained PET-PEG copolymers. Finally, the crystalline behaviour and fluorescence properties of obtained PET-PEG copolymer were systematically characterized with differential scanning calorimetry, polarized microscope and X-ray diffractometer as well as steady state and microscope coupled fluorescence spectroscopy.

Pure terephthalic acid (PTA), ethylene glycol (EG), isophthalic acid (IPA) and Sb2O3 were purchased from the Sinopec Yangzi petrochemical company (China), polyethylene glycol (average molecular weight of 1000) was obtained from Sanyo chemical industries (Japan), while phenol and 1, 1, 2, 2-tetrachloroethane were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (China).

PET-PEG copolymers were synthesized via the step polymerization of PTA, IPA, EG and PEG. Specifically, 13kg EG, 20kg PTA and 480 g IPA were put into a100 L pilot reactor protected by nitrogen, refluxed at 230°C with pressure at 2.7bars under constant stirring for 2.5h. Next, the produced water was separated from vaporized EG using a fractionating column and subsequently removed. Afterward, 4.2kg PEG (previously dried in a vacuum oven at 120°C for 1.5h) and10.2 g Sb2O3 as a catalyst were added into the reactor at atmospheric pressure. The temperature was then elevated to 280°C and the reaction continued under high vacuum for 3.5h, after which the melt was cooled in water to obtain the PET-PEG copolymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of carbon dots(CDs) formation via thermal decomposition of PEG
ce:section.ce:para: As the carbon precursor, 30g PEG was added into a 100mL three-neck flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer, followed by heating at 280°C and under nitrogen protection for a period of 3.5h. Meanwhile, samples were extracted at different time intervals and diluted with pure water for further measurements.

The UV-Vis absorption and steady state fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared CDs and the PET-PEG copolymers were recorded with a PerseeTU1901 UV-Vis spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The luminescence images of the copolymers were captured using a fluorescence microscopy (Eclipse TE2000-U, Nikon), while the hyperfluorescence spectroscopy was acquired from a microscope coupled fluorescence spectroscopy system of Ideaoptics co. Ltd. The crystalline behaviour of the PET and PET-PEG copolymers were characterized by means of a polarizing microscope (PLM, MP41, MShot), a X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-7000) operating at 40kV and 30mA using Cu-Kα radiation wavelength of 1.5406Å, and a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, Q100, TA), while their thermal stability was evaluated using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Q50, TA).

Initially, PEG was employed to enhance the toughness of PET thermoplastics by incorporating into polymer backbone structure. Interestingly, we observed the UV light excited intense blue emission from PEG solution after refluxing at ~280°C for certain time in nitrogen atmosphere, which were exactly the experimental conditions for copolymer synthesis. Furthermore, the detailed study of photophysical properties of PEG solution after refluxing for different time confirmed that the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) can be obtained during the thermal decomposition of PEG at high temperature in N2 atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 1a, an absorption peak around 245nm was detected after PEG refluxing more than 1.5h at 280°C, while no obvious absorption peak was recorded from 0.5h refluxing sample due to the incomplete growth of CDs. Next, the fluorescence emission spectra excited at different wavelengths of CDs when reacted for 0.5h and 2.0h were depicted in Fig. 1b and c, respectively (emission spectra of CDs sampled in other period were shown in Fig. S1 of supporting materials). For the sample refluxed 0.5h, a narrow emission peak with excitation wavelength of 300nm was detected when excitation wavelength was lower than 365nm (see Fig. 1b). On the contrary, the broad emission spectra can be excited using a wide range of wavelength from 300nm to 420nm for sample refluxed 2h, meanwhile the emission wavelength was gradually red-shifted as the increasing of excitation wavelength, this typical spectral feature was attributed to the existence of different sizes of CDs derived from the high-temperature pyrolysis process [13], which was also confirmed by the transmission electron microscopy images of obtained CDs as shown in Fig. S2. Furthermore, a red-shift of emission wavelength could be observed as increasing of reaction time as shown in Fig. 1d, which indicated that the size of CDs can be readily modulated by refluxing time of PEG at 280°C in N2.

Next, PEG was copolymerized with terephthalic acid dimethyl ester oligomer to obtain PET-PEG copolymers as shown in Fig. 2a, and the non-isothermal crystallization as well as the thermal decomposition behaviors of PET and PET-PEG were characterized with DSC and TGA, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, a decreasing of melting point up to 20°C and increasing of supercooling degree from 85°C to 96°C were observed after incorporating PEG into PET backbone, moreover a cold crystalline peak located at 119°C was only detected from PET-PEG copolymers. These results confirmed that the PET-PEG copolymer exhibited much slower crystalline process and less perfect macromolecular crystals compared to PET thermoplastics, which also resulted to the lower thermal stability of PET-PEG copolymer as shown in Fig. 2c.

Furthermore, the PLM and XRD techniques were employed to characterize the crystalline structures of synthesized polyesters. As shown in the PLM images, the well-defined spherulites with typical Maltase-Cross phenomenon were observed from PET thermoplastics ( Fig. 3a), while less regular crystals were imaged from PET-PEG copolymers in Fig. 3b, indicating the existence of lattice defects in this case. Moreover, PET and PET-PEG exhibited dramatically different X-ray diffraction patterns as demonstrated in Fig. 3c, where typical crystalline peaks located at 2θ values of 16.84°, 17.60°, 22.86°, and 26.35° were detected for PET, whilst a wide and disperse diffraction peak was observed for PET-PEG copolymer, further confirming the crystalline behaviour of PET can be effectively modulated by introducing flexible PEG chains into polymer backbones.

Finally, the fluorescence properties of the synthesized PET-PEG copolymers were explored as shown in Fig. 4A. The excitation wavelength (383nm) of copolymer was close to that of CDs, indicating the copolymer luminescence should derive from the CDs generated via the decomposition of PEG segment. In addition, the emission wavelength obtained from PET-PEG copolymer fiber was obviously red-shifted compared to that of solution phase, which would be attributed to the aggregation of CDs or the alternation of local refractive index. Meanwhile, the copolymer fiber subjected to different stretching degree were imaged with a fluorescence microscopy under bright field (a–d) and blue channel (e–h) as shown in Fig. 4B, it was clear that the intensive blue emission can be observed from copolymer fibers at different states.

In this work, we found that blue emitting CDs can be readily prepared via direct thermal decomposition of PEG in nitrogen atmosphere. Furthermore, PEG was incorporated into backbone of PET to obtain PET-PEG, where luminescent CDs were in-situ formulated and dispersed in obtained copolymers, which finally leaded to fluorescent polyester copolymers showing good toughness. The future work will dedicate on the optimization of CDs synthesis as well as correlation of copolymer crystalline and solid-state luminescence.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51403029) and the Scientific Research Foundation (LXHG5003) for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars from State Education Ministry.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.07.022.
